Dear Rachel:

This memorandum provides the monthly case status report for the above-referenced matter, covering activity during the reporting period of May 1 through May 31, 2025. Please note that this communication is protected by the attorney-client privilege and constitutes attorney work product prepared in anticipation of litigation. It should not be disclosed to any third party without prior consultation with this firm.

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

May 2025 was an exceptionally active month in the *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC* litigation. Three major workstreams dominated the team\'s efforts during the reporting period: (1) receipt and initial review of MedCore\'s third document production, which comprised approximately 85,000 documents and constituted the largest single production in the case to date; (2) the deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu, one of the three former Pinnacle data scientists at the center of this dispute, which took place on May 19, 2025; and (3) the filing of a motion to compel before Judge Stanton regarding MedCore\'s incomplete and evasive responses to Pinnacle\'s First Set of Interrogatories.

The convergence of these critical workstreams required the litigation team to operate at elevated capacity throughout the month. Mr. Hargrove\'s involvement was significantly more extensive than in prior months due to the critical nature of the Liu deposition, the strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house team, and his personal review of the third document production. Specifically, Mr. Hargrove devoted substantial personal attention to the matter this month, including attending the Liu deposition, conducting a comprehensive strategy session with the in-house team, and personally reviewing key documents in the third production. The Liu deposition was staffed to ensure comprehensive coverage given its critical importance to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, as Dr. Liu possesses direct knowledge of the proprietary algorithms at the heart of this case.

Looking ahead, the discovery close deadline remains September 30, 2025, leaving the team approximately four months to complete fact depositions, pursue any supplemental discovery compelled by the court, and initiate expert discovery. The sections below provide a detailed account of each workstream and the outlook for June and July.

**[II. Case Background and Current Posture]{.underline}**

For the monthly record, the following summarizes the current posture of the litigation. This action was filed by Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. against MedCore Analytics, LLC, a Georgia limited liability company headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, on September 15, 2024. Pinnacle\'s claims include breach of contract arising from non-disclosure and non-competition agreements, misappropriation of trade secrets under both the Georgia Trade Secrets Act and the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act (\"DTSA\"), 18 U.S.C. § 1836 *et seq.*, and unjust enrichment. The amount in controversy, as stated in Pinnacle\'s complaint, is approximately \$47.5 million in claimed damages.

MedCore, represented by Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, has asserted counterclaims against Pinnacle for tortious interference with business relations and seeks a declaratory judgment of non-infringement of any protectable trade secrets.

The core factual allegations center on MedCore\'s hiring of three former Pinnacle data scientists---including Dr. Rebecca Liu---and MedCore\'s alleged use of Pinnacle\'s proprietary patient-outcome prediction algorithms to develop competing analytics products. Pinnacle alleges that the departing employees transported proprietary data and source code to MedCore in violation of their contractual obligations.

Discovery commenced on February 3, 2025, pursuant to the scheduling order entered by Judge Stanton. The discovery close deadline is September 30, 2025. The case is currently in the fact discovery phase, with the team focused on document review, depositions of former Pinnacle employees now employed by MedCore, and interrogatory responses from both sides.

**[III. May 2025 Activity Report]{.underline}**

**[III.A. MedCore\'s Third Document Production]{.underline}**

In early May 2025, MedCore, through its counsel at Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, served its third document production in this matter. The production consisted of approximately 85,000 documents totaling over 1.2 million pages, making it by a significant margin the largest single production in the case to date. The prior two productions combined had totaled approximately 40,000 documents. The substantial increase in volume appears attributable to the production of internal MedCore email communications, technical specifications and design documents, source code documentation, and communications involving the three former Pinnacle data scientists, including extensive correspondence to and from Dr. Rebecca Liu regarding algorithm development at MedCore.

The production was hosted and processed by Tripoint Litigation Support LLC, the firm\'s e-discovery hosting vendor. Tripoint completed ingestion, de-duplication, and processing of the production by approximately May 6, at which point the documents were made available for attorney review on the Relativity platform.

Document review was conducted primarily by Marcus Tate, a contract attorney sourced through Redwood Document Solutions, the firm\'s contract attorney staffing vendor. Mr. Tate reviewed documents for relevance, responsiveness, and privilege throughout the month. James Pettersson supervised the document review workflow, established and refined search term protocols, and quality-checked privilege designations on an ongoing basis. On certain days when the volume of documents requiring review exceeded Mr. Tate\'s capacity, Tyler Webb assisted with document review to ensure the team remained on schedule.

David Hargrove personally reviewed a subset of the production documents that appeared most directly relevant to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, including technical memoranda authored by the former Pinnacle employees and algorithm-related correspondence between MedCore\'s development team and its product leadership. Mr. Hargrove undertook this review in early May to ensure that the team\'s overall approach to categorization, prioritization, and issue-tagging was properly calibrated to Pinnacle\'s litigation objectives. His direct involvement allowed the team to refine its review protocol and focus resources on the most consequential documents.

The volume of the third production contributed to elevated staffing needs throughout the month. In addition to the core review team, Nina Barros assisted with reviewing deposition transcripts from earlier depositions and conducted research on interrogatory-related issues that arose from the production documents. Priya Sengupta provided additional research and preparation support, particularly with respect to DTSA requirements relevant to categorizing the produced documents. The combined effort of these team members ensured that the review progressed efficiently and that key documents were identified promptly in advance of the Liu deposition and the motion to compel.

**[III.B. Deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu (May 19, 2025)]{.underline}**

Dr. Rebecca Liu, a former Pinnacle Senior Data Scientist and one of the three employees who departed to join MedCore, was deposed on May 19, 2025. Dr. Liu is a key fact witness in this matter. While employed at Pinnacle, she served as the lead developer of the proprietary patient-outcome prediction algorithms that form the basis of Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims. Pinnacle\'s complaint alleges that Dr. Liu transported proprietary data and source code to MedCore upon her departure and subsequently used that material to develop competing products. Her testimony was therefore anticipated to be among the most consequential in the entire case.

The deposition was taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia. Court reporting services were provided by Grayson Court Reporting Services. Opposing counsel from Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP represented Dr. Liu at the deposition.

Given Dr. Liu\'s status as the most critical fact witness in this matter and her direct knowledge of the algorithms at issue, Mr. Hargrove determined that his personal attendance was necessary to assess credibility and guide follow-up questioning in real time. Karen Cho conducted the direct examination of Dr. Liu. Mr. Hargrove attended the deposition to observe, provide real-time strategic guidance, and evaluate the witness\'s demeanor and credibility on the key factual issues. James Pettersson also attended to manage the exhibit presentation, take detailed notes, and prepare a comprehensive testimony summary for use in subsequent depositions and potential summary judgment briefing. All three attorneys were present for the full duration of the deposition.

The deposition lasted approximately 6.5 hours of on-the-record testimony and covered substantial ground. Without waiving any privilege, we can report at a general level that Dr. Liu testified regarding her role in developing the patient-outcome algorithms at Pinnacle, the circumstances and reasons for her departure from Pinnacle, the nature of the files and materials she retained or accessed after her departure, and the scope and nature of her work at MedCore. Certain testimony was significant and potentially favorable to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, particularly with respect to Dr. Liu\'s acknowledgment of her familiarity with specific proprietary methodologies. Other portions of the testimony raised areas requiring follow-up in subsequent depositions of the other former Pinnacle employees and certain MedCore personnel.

A post-deposition debrief was conducted the same afternoon with the litigation team to assess the testimony, identify areas of strength and concern, and plan follow-up discovery. Karen Cho subsequently conferred with Rachel Dominguez by telephone on May 20 regarding the implications of Dr. Liu\'s testimony for the overall case strategy.

In preparation for the deposition, Mr. Hargrove had participated in a May 8 team strategy conference call to discuss the overall discovery strategy and deposition preparation for the former Pinnacle employee witnesses. That call, which included the full litigation team, addressed the planned line of questioning for Dr. Liu, the prioritization of document review in the third production to support the deposition, and coordination of interrogatory response deadlines. Mr. Hargrove also spent time during the week of May 12 reviewing key documents and outlining strategic themes for the examination. This preparatory work, combined with his attendance at the deposition itself, reflected a level of partner engagement commensurate with the significance of Dr. Liu as a witness.

**[III.C. Motion to Compel --- MedCore\'s Incomplete Interrogatory Responses]{.underline}**

In late May 2025, Hargrove & Linden filed a Motion to Compel on behalf of Pinnacle before Judge Stanton. The motion concerns MedCore\'s incomplete and evasive responses to Pinnacle\'s First Set of Interrogatories (Nos. 1 through 25), which were served on MedCore in March 2025. MedCore\'s responses, received in April, were deficient in several material respects.

The key deficiencies identified in MedCore\'s responses include: (a) the interposition of boilerplate objections---including overbreadth, undue burden, and relevance objections---without providing substantive answers to the underlying questions; (b) failure to identify individuals with knowledge of the algorithm development process at MedCore, despite interrogatories specifically requesting such identification; and (c) incomplete and non-responsive answers regarding MedCore\'s revenue derived from products utilizing the allegedly misappropriated algorithms, which is directly relevant to Pinnacle\'s damages claims.

The motion brief was primarily drafted by Karen Cho. Tyler Webb performed legal research and cite-checking in support of the brief. James Pettersson prepared the exhibits to the motion, including annotated copies of the deficient interrogatory responses, and assisted with drafting certain sections of the brief addressing the factual background. Prior to filing, a meet-and-confer communication was exchanged with Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP as required under the Local Rules, but opposing counsel was unwilling to supplement the responses.

The motion is currently pending before Judge Stanton and no hearing date has yet been scheduled. Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP has indicated through correspondence that it intends to oppose the motion.

**[III.D. Interrogatory Responses and Written Discovery]{.underline}**

In parallel with the motion to compel and the deposition, the team continued preparing Pinnacle\'s own responses to MedCore\'s interrogatories and requests for production related to MedCore\'s tortious interference counterclaim and its requests regarding Pinnacle\'s damages methodology. James Pettersson took primary responsibility for drafting these responses, with review and input from Karen Cho to ensure consistency with the overall litigation strategy.

Nina Barros assisted with research on trade secret preemption issues that arose in connection with certain of MedCore\'s interrogatories, which sought information that Pinnacle contends is preempted by its trade secret claims. Priya Sengupta conducted legal research on DTSA requirements relevant to the specificity of trade secret identification in interrogatory responses, an issue that has been the subject of recent case law developments in the Eleventh Circuit.

The team also coordinated with Pinnacle\'s internal IT department and data science team to gather the factual information needed to respond to MedCore\'s technical interrogatories regarding the development history and architecture of the patient-outcome algorithms. This coordination required several telephone calls and email exchanges with Pinnacle personnel during the second and third weeks of May.

**[III.E. Team Strategy and Client Communications]{.underline}**

Two significant strategy sessions took place during May 2025. First, on May 8, the full litigation team participated in a strategy conference call to discuss overall discovery strategy and deposition preparation for the remaining former Pinnacle employee witnesses. The call covered the planned approach for the Liu deposition, the prioritization of document review within the third production to identify documents most relevant to the upcoming depositions, and coordination of deadlines for outstanding interrogatory responses. The call also addressed staffing allocation for the remainder of the discovery period.

Second, on May 27, David Hargrove conducted an in-person strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house legal team---Rachel Dominguez and Sandra Whitmore---at Pinnacle\'s offices at 2800 Peachtree Road NE, Suite 1400, Atlanta, Georgia 30305. The session covered several important strategic topics, including the timeline and viability of a potential summary judgment motion following the close of discovery, the retention of expert witnesses for both damages quantification and technical analysis of the algorithm code, and the overall litigation strategy through the September 30, 2025 discovery cutoff. Following the session, Mr. Hargrove drafted an outline for the expert discovery plan, identifying categories of expertise needed, a proposed timeline for retention and report preparation, and preliminary thoughts on potential expert candidates.

In addition to these formal sessions, Karen Cho maintained regular communication with Rachel Dominguez throughout the month regarding case developments, deposition scheduling, and coordination of document collection efforts within Pinnacle.

**[IV. Staffing and Resource Update]{.underline}**

The following team members worked on the *Pinnacle v. MedCore* matter during May 2025:

> • **David Hargrove, Lead Partner** --- Overall case strategy, attendance at the Liu deposition, in-person client strategy session on May 27, personal review of key documents in the third production, and participation in the May 8 team strategy call. Mr. Hargrove\'s involvement was more extensive than in typical months, reflecting the convergence of several critical workstreams---the Liu deposition, the strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house team, and his review of key documents in the third production. We anticipate that his hours will return to more typical levels in June and July as the deposition schedule shifts to more junior witnesses and the third-production review approaches completion.
>
> • **Karen Cho, Senior Associate** --- Day-to-day case management, conducted the direct examination at the Liu deposition, drafted the motion to compel, coordinated with opposing counsel at Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, and maintained regular communication with Pinnacle\'s in-house team.
>
> • **James Pettersson, Mid-Level Associate** --- Supervised document review workflow and quality-checked privilege designations, drafted Pinnacle\'s interrogatory responses, attended the Liu deposition to manage exhibits and prepare the testimony summary, and assisted with the motion to compel.
>
> • **Nina Barros, Mid-Level Associate** --- Assisted with interrogatory-related research on trade secret preemption issues and reviewed deposition transcripts. Her involvement this month was consistent with the request submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025.
>
> • **Tyler Webb, Junior Associate** --- Legal research and cite-checking for the motion to compel brief, and assisted with document review on days of elevated volume.
>
> • **Priya Sengupta, Junior Associate** --- Legal research on DTSA requirements relevant to interrogatory responses, deposition exhibit binder preparation, and cite-checking.
>
> • **Marcus Tate, Contract Attorney** (via Redwood Document Solutions) --- Primary document reviewer for the third production batch, reviewing for relevance, responsiveness, and privilege.

In total, seven timekeepers billed time to this matter during May 2025. The elevated staffing reflects the volume of work generated by the concurrent workstreams described above. As noted, we expect staffing levels and partner hours to moderate in the coming months as the most intensive phase of the third-production review concludes and the deposition schedule proceeds with witnesses of comparatively lesser complexity.

**[V. Upcoming Work --- June--July 2025 Outlook]{.underline}**

**Depositions.** Two additional depositions of former Pinnacle data scientists---the remaining two of the three employees who departed to join MedCore---are scheduled for June and early July 2025, respectively. These depositions will build on the themes developed in the Liu deposition. Depositions of MedCore corporate personnel are expected to follow in July and August 2025.

**Document Review.** Continued review and analysis of the approximately 85,000-document third production will extend through mid-to-late June, given the volume of material and the need to complete privilege review before the production documents can be fully utilized in depositions and briefing.

**Motion to Compel.** We anticipate a ruling or hearing on the pending motion to compel in June. If the motion is granted in whole or in part, we expect to receive supplemental interrogatory responses from MedCore that may yield significant additional information regarding MedCore\'s use of the algorithms and associated revenue.

**Expert Discovery.** Following the May 27 strategy session, Hargrove & Linden will work with Pinnacle to identify and retain expert witnesses for damages quantification and technical analysis of the proprietary algorithms. Given the September 30 discovery cutoff, expert reports will likely need to be completed by mid-to-late August 2025, which makes the prompt engagement of experts in June a priority.

**MedCore Counterclaim Discovery.** Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP has indicated that it intends to serve additional discovery requests related to MedCore\'s tortious interference counterclaim. The team will need to prepare Pinnacle\'s responses and coordinate document collection with Pinnacle\'s internal teams.

**General Outlook.** We anticipate that June will be a similarly active month given the remaining deposition schedule and ongoing document review, though we expect total hours to moderate somewhat as the third-production review approaches completion.

**[VI. Key Deadlines and Calendar]{.underline}**

  **Discovery Close Deadline**                               **September 30, 2025**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Approximately four months remain in the discovery period. We will continue to monitor deadlines closely and will promptly advise Pinnacle of any scheduling developments.

**[VII. Closing and Action Items]{.underline}**

We respectfully request your assistance with the following items in the near term:

1\. **Expert Witness Identification.** Please coordinate with Pinnacle\'s data science team to identify potential expert witnesses who may be qualified to provide technical analysis of the patient-outcome algorithms at issue. We would appreciate receiving any recommendations or internal contacts by mid-June so that we can begin the retention process promptly.

2\. **Counterclaim Document Compilation.** In anticipation of MedCore\'s additional discovery requests regarding its tortious interference counterclaim, please begin compiling internal documents and communications related to Pinnacle\'s competitive practices and any interactions with MedCore\'s clients or prospective clients. Early identification of responsive materials will allow us to prepare timely and thorough responses.

3\. **May 2025 Invoice.** The May 2025 invoice (Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN) will be submitted under separate cover in the coming days.

We would be happy to schedule a call at your convenience to discuss any questions regarding the May activity described in this report or the upcoming work plan for June and July. Please do not hesitate to reach out with any questions. We look forward to continuing to advance Pinnacle\'s interests in this matter.

{"file_path": "memo-draft.md", "content": "# INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**TO:** Rachel Dominguez, Esq., Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel)
**CC:** Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel
**FROM:** Legal Billing Review, Office of the General Counsel
**DATE:** June 12, 2025
**RE:** Review of Outside Counsel Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN — Hargrove & Linden LLP — *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.) — Billing Period May 1–31, 2025

---

## I. Executive Summary

Hargrove & Linden LLP ("H&L" or the "Firm") submitted Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, dated June 5, 2025, for legal services rendered during May 2025 in the above-captioned matter. The invoice seeks **$201,822.50**, consisting of **$193,372.50** in attorney fees (422.5 hours across seven timekeepers) and **$8,450.00** in expenses.

This review was conducted against (i) the Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan dated February 10, 2025 (the "Staffing Plan"); (ii) Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines, effective January 1, 2023 (the "Billing Guidelines"); (iii) the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing email exchange between Karen Cho and Rachel Dominguez regarding the proposed addition of Nina Barros; and (iv) H&L's Monthly Case Status Report for May 2025, dated June 1, 2025 (the "Status Report").

The invoice contains multiple, material Billing Guidelines violations. **We recommend a total reduction of $72,440.01, reducing the amount payable from $201,822.50 to $129,382.49** (a reduction of approximately 35.9%). The principal findings are:

1. **Two unapproved timekeepers.** Nina Barros (45.0 hrs / $21,375.00) and Priya Sengupta (27.5 hrs / $10,312.50) billed without the written pre-approval required by Billing Guidelines § 4.2 and the Staffing Plan. Barros's addition was *requested* but expressly *not approved*; Sengupta was added with no request to Pinnacle at all. **Recommended: full rejection — $31,687.50.**

2. **Contract attorney rate exceeds both the rate cap and the approved rate.** Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr, exceeding the § 5.1 cap of $200/hr and the Staffing Plan's approved pass-through rate of $185/hr. **Recommended: re-rate to $185/hr — $3,000.00.**

3. **Pervasive block billing.** Forty-four (44) time entries combine two or more discrete tasks in a single line item, in violation of § 5.4. The automatic 25% reduction applies to each. **Recommended: $27,050.01.**

4. **Task/timekeeper mismatches (§ 6.1).** A partner performed document review and cite-checking, a mid-level associate performed administrative scheduling, and a junior associate performed routine first-level document review. **Recommended: $5,290.00.**

5. **Over-staffed internal meeting and deposition (§ 4.3).** Five timekeepers billed a single May 8 strategy call (cap is three) and three attorneys billed the Liu deposition without advance approval for the third. **Recommended: $3,312.50.**

6. **Non-reimbursable travel expense (§§ 6.4, 8.2, 8.3).** $2,100.00 in "travel" for two firm attorneys to attend a deposition held at the Firm's own Atlanta office is local travel and was not pre-approved. **Recommended: $2,100.00.**

7. **Budget overage without pre-approval (§ 7.3).** Attorney fees of $193,372.50 exceed the 115% threshold ($189,750.00) with no pre-approval. The violation is flagged; because the specific reductions above bring fees below the cap, no *additional* dollar reduction is required under § 7.3, though Pinnacle reserves the right to enforce the cap.

8. **Missing budget-to-actual reconciliation (§§ 7.2, 9.1(g)).** The invoice omits the required budget-to-actual reconciliation and variance narrative. The Firm should resubmit with the reconciliation.

The detailed findings, citations, and calculations supporting each reduction follow.

---

## V. Detailed Findings

### A. Unapproved Timekeepers (Billing Guidelines § 4.2; Staffing Plan §§ 2.3, 2.4, 4)

**Standard.** § 4.2 requires that "[a]ny additions to, or substitutions on, the Approved Staffing Plan … must be pre-approved in writing by Pinnacle's Designated In-House Counsel prior to any such timekeeper performing billable work." Staffing changes submitted after the fact "are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion." The Staffing Plan (§ 4) reiterates that the listed timekeepers are the "complete and exclusive" roster and that any new timekeeper requires "prior written approval." The Staffing Plan further provides that "only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time," and that James Pettersson "is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster."

#### 1. Nina Barros — Mid-Level Associate (45.0 hrs / $21,375.00) — Full Rejection

Barros is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Staffing Plan expressly states that Pettersson is the only authorized mid-level associate. On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Rachel Dominguez requesting Barros's addition. Rachel Dominguez's reply of May 1, 2025 stated: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you. … I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* No further approval was given. The Firm's own Rate Schedule on the invoice concedes the status: "Nina Barros … **Pending Approval — Added May 2025** … client response pending."

The Firm nonetheless billed Barros for 45.0 hours ($21,375.00) throughout May. The Status Report's assertion that Barros's involvement "was consistent with the request submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025" misstates the standard: a *request* is not *approval*. § 4.2 is unambiguous that "the absence of a response shall not constitute approval." No exigent-circumstance notification under § 4.2 was made (the request was a routine staffing addition, and Pinnacle expressly asked for time to review). The Firm proceeded at its own risk.

**Recommendation:** Reject all Barros fees in full — **$21,375.00**. Pinnacle may, in its discretion under § 4.2, consider retroactive approval for a portion if the Firm submits a written justification, but as billed the fees are not payable.

#### 2. Priya Sengupta — Junior Associate (27.5 hrs / $10,312.50) — Full Rejection

Sengupta is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Staffing Plan authorizes a single junior-associate slot, filled by Tyler Webb (2nd year), and states that assigning "more than one junior associate concurrently would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under § 4.2." Sengupta (1st year) was added in May and billed concurrently with Webb for 27.5 hours ($10,312.50). Unlike Barros, there is **no record of any staffing-change request to Pinnacle** for Sengupta — she was simply "added May 2025" (per the invoice Rate Schedule) with no request, no justification, and no approval. This is a more straightforward violation than Barros: no request was even submitted.

**Recommendation:** Reject all Sengupta fees in full — **$10,312.50**.

**Combined unapproved-timekeeper reduction: $31,687.50.** (Note: several Barros and Sengupta entries are also block-billed; the full rejection for unapproved status supersedes, and no separate block-billing reduction is applied to those entries to avoid double-counting.)

---

### B. Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Cap and Approved Rate (§ 5.1; Staffing Plan § 2.5)

Marcus Tate, the contract attorney sourced through Redwood Document Solutions, was billed at **$210/hr**. This rate exceeds (i) the § 5.1 / Appendix A cap for "Contract Attorney / Staff Attorney" of **$200/hr**, and (ii) the **$185/hr** pass-through rate approved in the Staffing Plan (§ 2.5 and § 6 Rate Schedule Confirmation), which the Staffing Plan describes as "pass-through at vendor rates with no markup." § 5.1 provides that "[r]ates in excess of these caps will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap upon invoice review." The invoice's own Rate Schedule mislabels $210 as "Approved," but the controlling approved document (the Staffing Plan) sets the rate at $185.

**Calculation.** Reduce 120.0 hours from $210/hr to the approved rate of $185/hr (which is below the $200 cap and therefore satisfies both the cap and the approved-rate obligation): 120 × ($210 − $185) = **$3,000.00**. (Even a reduction only to the $200 cap would yield $1,200.00; the further reduction to the contractually approved $185 is warranted because Pinnacle never agreed to more than $185.)

**Recommendation: $3,000.00.**

---

### C. Block Billing (§ 5.4) — 44 Entries

**Standard.** § 5.4 prohibits time entries that "combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line item without separately allocating time to each task." A "discrete task" is "a distinct activity that could be independently described and timed, such as: drafting a motion, reviewing a specific set of documents … attending a deposition, conducting legal research on a specific issue, or participating in a conference call." The Guideline's own examples treat "confer with partner regarding strategy" and "prepare post-deposition summary" as discrete tasks separate from research, drafting, or deposition attendance. The remedy is "an automatic twenty-five percent (25%) reduction to the total fees of each block-billed entry," which "is in addition to, and not in lieu of, any other reductions." The Firm may avoid the reduction by resubmitting "as separate line items with individually allocated time within fifteen (15) days."

**Finding.** Forty-four (44) entries by approved timekeepers combine two or more discrete tasks. The pattern is pervasive: drafting combined with research; review/revision combined with a separate conference; deposition attendance combined with a post-deposition summary; brief finalization combined with administrative filing coordination. ---

### D. Task/Timekeeper Appropriateness (§ 6.1)

**Standard.** § 6.1 requires that each task be assigned to "the lowest-cost timekeeper capable of competently performing the work." Document review and cite-checking by partners or senior associates are "presumptively inappropriate" and subject to reduction to the appropriate lower-level rate (§ 6.1(a), (b)). Routine first-level document review by a junior associate is reduced to the contract attorney rate (§ 6.1(a)). Administrative tasks (scheduling, coordinating with court reporters, calendaring) are "presumptively inappropriate for any attorney" and are to be written off or reduced to the paralegal rate (§ 6.1(g)–(k); § 2).

#### 1. Entry 9 — Partner document review (Hargrove, 4.5 hrs / $4,432.50)

"Review and analyze documents produced by MedCore in third production batch; prepare detailed memorandum summarizing key findings for client." This is document review by the lead partner. § 6.1(a) directs that document review by a partner be "reduced to the contract attorney rate ($200/hr cap) or the junior associate rate, depending on the nature of the review and whether the exercise of legal judgment was required." The Status Report indicates the review involved legal judgment (calibrating the review protocol and identifying key documents for trade-secret claims), so reduction to the **junior associate rate ($375/hr)** is appropriate rather than the contract-attorney rate.

**Calculation:** 4.5 × ($985 − $375) = **$2,745.00.**

#### 2. Entry 43 — Partner cite-checking (Hargrove, 2.0 hrs / $1,970.00)

"Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief." Cite-checking is a Routine Task (§ 2) and is "presumptively inappropriate" for a partner (§ 6.1(b)), to be reduced to the junior associate rate. (Junior associates Webb and Sengupta performed the underlying cite-checking on this same brief — Entries 47 and 54 — confirming this is junior-level work.)

**Calculation:** 2.0 × ($985 − $375) = **$1,220.00.**

#### 3. Entry 15 — Administrative tasks by an attorney (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs / $712.50)

"Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations." This is entirely administrative — scheduling (§ 6.1(g)), coordinating with a court reporter (§ 6.1(h)), and confirming locations (§ 6.1(k)). Administrative tasks "are considered overhead expenses of Outside Counsel and shall not be billed to Pinnacle at any rate." The entry should be written off in full (Pinnacle's discretion under § 6.1 to reduce to the $175/hr paralegal rate would yield $262.50; full write-off is recommended).

**Recommendation: $712.50** (full write-off).

#### 4. Entry 79 — Junior associate routine document review (Webb, 3.5 hrs / $1,312.50)

"Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege." This is routine first-level document review. § 6.1(a) provides that "[d]ocument review by a junior associate that constitutes routine first-level review for relevance or responsiveness → reduced to the contract attorney rate." The Status Report confirms Webb "assisted with document review" on days when volume exceeded the contract attorney's capacity — i.e., routine first-level review. Reduce to the contract attorney rate ($200/hr cap).

**Calculation:** 3.5 × ($375 − $200) = **$612.50.**

**Combined § 6.1 reduction: $5,290.00.**

### E. Conference Attendance and Deposition Staffing (§ 4.3)

**Standard.** § 4.3 limits billing timekeepers for "any single internal meeting or conference call" to **three (3)**, even for Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions; excess attendees must record time as non-billable. For depositions, two billing attorneys are permitted without approval; a third requires **advance approval** requested "no later than five (5) business days before the scheduled deposition," and "[i]n the absence of such advance approval, Pinnacle reserves the right to reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

#### 1. May 8 team strategy call — five billing timekeepers (excess of two)

Five timekeepers billed the same May 8 strategy call: Hargrove (3.0, Entry 22), Cho (3.0, Entry 23), Pettersson (3.0, Entry 24), Barros (2.5, Entry 25), and Webb (2.5, Entry 26). The cap is three. Two attendees are excess. Barros is already fully rejected (§ A.1). Of the four *approved* timekeepers who billed, one is excess. No advance designation of billing attendees was made. Consistent with the "lowest-cost timekeeper" principle, the three most senior/essential attendees (Hargrove, Cho, Pettersson) are retained, and the most junior excess attendee — **Webb (2.5 hrs / $937.50, Entry 26)** — is disallowed.

**Recommendation: $937.50.**

#### 2. Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition (May 19) — three billing attorneys without approval for the third

Three attorneys billed the Liu deposition: Hargrove (4.0, Entry 60), Cho (6.5, Entry 61), and Pettersson (5.0, Entry 62). Two deposition attorneys are permitted without approval; a third requires advance approval five business days before the deposition. No such approval was requested or given. Per the Status Report, Cho conducted the direct examination and Hargrove attended to observe and provide real-time strategic guidance — the two primary attorneys. Pettersson attended "to manage the exhibit presentation, take detailed notes, and prepare a comprehensive testimony summary," i.e., the additional (third) timekeeper. § 4.3 permits Pinnacle to "reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

Pettersson's Entry 62 ("Attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu; prepare summary of testimony and identify key admissions …") is predominantly deposition-attendance time and is block-billed so that the attendance portion cannot be isolated. Because Pettersson is the unapproved third deposition attorney and the entry is chiefly deposition attendance, the entry is reduced in its entirety. (The Firm may seek retroactive approval under § 4.2/§ 10.4 or resubmit any genuinely separate post-deposition summary time as a discrete entry for reconsideration; no block-billing reduction is separately applied to Entry 62 to avoid double-counting.)

**Calculation:** 5.0 × $475 = **$2,375.00.**

**Combined § 4.3 reduction: $3,312.50.**

### F. Monthly Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval (§ 7.3)

**Standard.** § 7.3 requires pre-approval if monthly attorney fees are projected to exceed the high end of the approved range by more than 15%. The Staffing Plan (§ 3) set the approved range at **$95,000–$165,000**, so the 115% ceiling is **$165,000 × 1.15 = $189,750.00**. Fees exceeding $189,750 without pre-approval may be reduced to that ceiling.

**Finding.** As-billed attorney fees total **$193,372.50**, which exceeds the $189,750.00 ceiling by **$3,622.50**. No pre-approval was sought or given (the only staffing/budget communication in the record is the Barros email, in which Ms. Dominguez stated she wanted to "check where things stand against the current budget and staffing plan"). This is a § 7.3 violation.

**Treatment.** § 7.3 expressly provides that budget-compliance reductions "do not offset or preclude" the other Guidelines reductions. The specific reductions in Sections A–E above reduce payable attorney fees to **$123,032.49**, which is *below* the $189,750.00 ceiling. Accordingly, the § 7.3 cap is not the binding constraint, and **no additional dollar reduction is required under § 7.3** at this time. Pinnacle nonetheless **reserves the right to enforce the $189,750.00 ceiling**, and the violation should be documented and addressed: the Firm must obtain pre-approval before any future month exceeds the ceiling and should consider submitting a phase-budget adjustment request under § 7.4 if the elevated discovery volume is expected to persist.

Travel to one's own office is local travel. The expense fails on three independent grounds:

§ 9.1(g) requires each invoice to include "[a] budget-to-actual reconciliation as required by § 7.2, showing both the current period and cumulative figures." § 7.2 further requires a "narrative explanation of any variance greater than ten percent (10%) from the midpoint of the approved monthly range." The approved midpoint is $130,000; as-billed fees of $193,372.50 represent a **~48.7% variance** above midpoint. The invoice contains no budget-to-actual reconciliation and no variance narrative. 3. **Time-entry narratives (§ 9.2).** Narratives are generally adequately specific. Entry 79 ("Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege") is generic compared to the contract attorney's Bates-range-specific entries but identifies the production batch and is not so vague as to warrant rejection.

## V. Detailed Findings

### A. Unapproved Timekeepers (Billing Guidelines § 4.2; Staffing Plan §§ 2.3, 2.4, 4)

**Standard.** § 4.2 requires that "[a]ny additions to, or substitutions on, the Approved Staffing Plan … must be pre-approved in writing by Pinnacle's Designated In-House Counsel prior to any such timekeeper performing billable work." Staffing changes submitted after the fact "are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion." The Staffing Plan (§ 4) reiterates that the listed timekeepers are the "complete and exclusive" roster and that any new timekeeper requires "prior written approval." The Staffing Plan further provides that "only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time," and that James Pettersson "is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster."

#### 1. Nina Barros — Mid-Level Associate (45.0 hrs / $21,375.00) — Full Rejection

Barros is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Staffing Plan expressly states that Pettersson is the only authorized mid-level associate. On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Rachel Dominguez requesting Barros's addition. Rachel Dominguez's reply of May 1, 2025 stated: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you. … I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* No further approval was given. The Firm's own Rate Schedule on the invoice concedes the status: "Nina Barros … **Pending Approval — Added May 2025** … client response pending."

The Firm nonetheless billed Barros for 45.0 hours ($21,375.00) throughout May. The Status Report's assertion that Barros's involvement "was consistent with the request submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025" misstates the standard: a *request* is not *approval*. § 4.2 is unambiguous that "the absence of a response shall not constitute approval." No exigent-circumstance notification under § 4.2 was made (the request was a routine staffing addition, and Pinnacle expressly asked for time to review). The Firm proceeded at its own risk.

**Recommendation:** Reject all Barros fees in full — **$21,375.00**. Pinnacle may, in its discretion under § 4.2, consider retroactive approval for a portion if the Firm submits a written justification, but as billed the fees are not payable.

#### 2. Priya Sengupta — Junior Associate (27.5 hrs / $10,312.50) — Full Rejection

Sengupta is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Staffing Plan authorizes a single junior-associate slot, filled by Tyler Webb (2nd year), and states that assigning "more than one junior associate concurrently would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under § 4.2." Sengupta (1st year) was added in May and billed concurrently with Webb for 27.5 hours ($10,312.50). Unlike Barros, there is **no record of any staffing-change request to Pinnacle** for Sengupta — she was simply "added May 2025" (per the invoice Rate Schedule) with no request, no justification, and no approval. This is a more straightforward violation than Barros: no request was even submitted.

**Recommendation:** Reject all Sengupta fees in full — **$10,312.50**.

**Combined unapproved-timekeeper reduction: $31,687.50.** (Note: several Barros and Sengupta entries are also block-billed; the full rejection for unapproved status supersedes, and no separate block-billing reduction is applied to those entries to avoid double-counting.)

### B. Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Cap and Approved Rate (§ 5.1; Staffing Plan § 2.5)

Marcus Tate, the contract attorney sourced through Redwood Document Solutions, was billed at **$210/hr**. This rate exceeds (i) the § 5.1 / Appendix A cap for "Contract Attorney / Staff Attorney" of **$200/hr**, and (ii) the **$185/hr** pass-through rate approved in the Staffing Plan (§ 2.5 and § 6 Rate Schedule Confirmation), which the Staffing Plan describes as "pass-through at vendor rates with no markup." § 5.1 provides that "[r]ates in excess of these caps will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap upon invoice review." The invoice's own Rate Schedule mislabels $210 as "Approved," but the controlling approved document (the Staffing Plan) sets the rate at $185.

**Calculation.** Reduce 120.0 hours from $210/hr to the approved rate of $185/hr (which is below the $200 cap and therefore satisfies both the cap and the approved-rate obligation): 120 × ($210 − $185) = **$3,000.00**. (Even a reduction only to the $200 cap would yield $1,200.00; the further reduction to the contractually approved $185 is warranted because Pinnacle never agreed to more than $185.)

**Recommendation: $3,000.00.**

### C. Block Billing (§ 5.4) — 44 Entries

**Standard.** § 5.4 prohibits time entries that "combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line item without separately allocating time to each task." A "discrete task" is "a distinct activity that could be independently described and timed, such as: drafting a motion, reviewing a specific set of documents … attending a deposition, conducting legal research on a specific issue, or participating in a conference call." The Guideline's own examples treat "confer with partner regarding strategy" and "prepare post-deposition summary" as discrete tasks separate from research, drafting, or deposition attendance. The remedy is "an automatic twenty-five percent (25%) reduction to the total fees of each block-billed entry," which "is in addition to, and not in lieu of, any other reductions." The Firm may avoid the reduction by resubmitting "as separate line items with individually allocated time within fifteen (15) days."

- **Entry 61 (Cho, 6.5 hrs):** "Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu …; post-deposition analysis and team debrief." — Three discrete tasks (preparation; deposition attendance; post-deposition analysis/debrief), mirroring the Guideline's own impermissible example.

---

### D. Task/Timekeeper Appropriateness (§ 6.1)

**Standard.** § 6.1 requires that each task be assigned to "the lowest-cost timekeeper capable of competently performing the work." Document review and cite-checking by partners or senior associates are "presumptively inappropriate" and subject to reduction to the appropriate lower-level rate (§ 6.1(a), (b)). Routine first-level document review by a junior associate is reduced to the contract attorney rate (§ 6.1(a)). Administrative tasks (scheduling, coordinating with court reporters, calendaring) are "presumptively inappropriate for any attorney" and are to be written off or reduced to the paralegal rate (§ 6.1(g)–(k); § 2).

#### 1. Entry 9 — Partner document review (Hargrove, 4.5 hrs / $4,432.50)

"Review and analyze documents produced by MedCore in third production batch; prepare detailed memorandum summarizing key findings for client." This is document review by the lead partner. § 6.1(a) directs that document review by a partner be "reduced to the contract attorney rate ($200/hr cap) or the junior associate rate, depending on the nature of the review and whether the exercise of legal judgment was required." The Status Report indicates the review involved legal judgment (calibrating the review protocol and identifying key documents for trade-secret claims), so reduction to the **junior associate rate ($375/hr)** is appropriate rather than the contract-attorney rate.

**Calculation:** 4.5 × ($985 − $375) = **$2,745.00.**

#### 2. Entry 43 — Partner cite-checking (Hargrove, 2.0 hrs / $1,970.00)

"Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief." Cite-checking is a Routine Task (§ 2) and is "presumptively inappropriate" for a partner (§ 6.1(b)), to be reduced to the junior associate rate. (Junior associates Webb and Sengupta performed the underlying cite-checking on this same brief — Entries 47 and 54 — confirming this is junior-level work.)

**Calculation:** 2.0 × ($985 − $375) = **$1,220.00.**

#### 3. Entry 15 — Administrative tasks by an attorney (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs / $712.50)

"Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations." This is entirely administrative — scheduling (§ 6.1(g)), coordinating with a court reporter (§ 6.1(h)), and confirming locations (§ 6.1(k)). Administrative tasks "are considered overhead expenses of Outside Counsel and shall not be billed to Pinnacle at any rate." The entry should be written off in full (Pinnacle's discretion under § 6.1 to reduce to the $175/hr paralegal rate would yield $262.50; full write-off is recommended).

**Recommendation: $712.50** (full write-off).

#### 4. Entry 79 — Junior associate routine document review (Webb, 3.5 hrs / $1,312.50)

"Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege." This is routine first-level document review. § 6.1(a) provides that "[d]ocument review by a junior associate that constitutes routine first-level review for relevance or responsiveness → reduced to the contract attorney rate." The Status Report confirms Webb "assisted with document review" on days when volume exceeded the contract attorney's capacity — i.e., routine first-level review. Reduce to the contract attorney rate ($200/hr cap).

**Calculation:** 3.5 × ($375 − $200) = **$612.50.**

**Combined § 6.1 reduction: $5,290.00.**

---

### E. Conference Attendance and Deposition Staffing (§ 4.3)

**Standard.** § 4.3 limits billing timekeepers for "any single internal meeting or conference call" to **three (3)**, even for Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions; excess attendees must record time as non-billable. For depositions, two billing attorneys are permitted without approval; a third requires **advance approval** requested "no later than five (5) business days before the scheduled deposition," and "[i]n the absence of such advance approval, Pinnacle reserves the right to reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

#### 1. May 8 team strategy call — five billing timekeepers (excess of two)

Five timekeepers billed the same May 8 strategy call: Hargrove (3.0, Entry 22), Cho (3.0, Entry 23), Pettersson (3.0, Entry 24), Barros (2.5, Entry 25), and Webb (2.5, Entry 26). The cap is three. Two attendees are excess. Barros is already fully rejected (§ A.1). Of the four *approved* timekeepers who billed, one is excess. No advance designation of billing attendees was made. Consistent with the "lowest-cost timekeeper" principle, the three most senior/essential attendees (Hargrove, Cho, Pettersson) are retained, and the most junior excess attendee — **Webb (2.5 hrs / $937.50, Entry 26)** — is disallowed.

**Recommendation: $937.50.**

#### 2. Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition (May 19) — three billing attorneys without approval for the third

Three attorneys billed the Liu deposition: Hargrove (4.0, Entry 60), Cho (6.5, Entry 61), and Pettersson (5.0, Entry 62). Two deposition attorneys are permitted without approval; a third requires advance approval five business days before the deposition. No such approval was requested or given. Per the Status Report, Cho conducted the direct examination and Hargrove attended to observe and provide real-time strategic guidance — the two primary attorneys. Pettersson attended "to manage the exhibit presentation, take detailed notes, and prepare a comprehensive testimony summary," i.e., the additional (third) timekeeper. § 4.3 permits Pinnacle to "reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

Pettersson's Entry 62 ("Attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu; prepare summary of testimony and identify key admissions …") is predominantly deposition-attendance time and is block-billed so that the attendance portion cannot be isolated. Because Pettersson is the unapproved third deposition attorney and the entry is chiefly deposition attendance, the entry is reduced in its entirety. (The Firm may seek retroactive approval under § 4.2/§ 10.4 or resubmit any genuinely separate post-deposition summary time as a discrete entry for reconsideration; no block-billing reduction is separately applied to Entry 62 to avoid double-counting.)

**Calculation:** 5.0 × $475 = **$2,375.00.**

**Combined § 4.3 reduction: $3,312.50.**

---

### F. Monthly Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval (§ 7.3)

**Standard.** § 7.3 requires pre-approval if monthly attorney fees are projected to exceed the high end of the approved range by more than 15%. The Staffing Plan (§ 3) set the approved range at **$95,000–$165,000**, so the 115% ceiling is **$165,000 × 1.15 = $189,750.00**. Fees exceeding $189,750 without pre-approval may be reduced to that ceiling.

**Finding.** As-billed attorney fees total **$193,372.50**, which exceeds the $189,750.00 ceiling by **$3,622.50**. No pre-approval was sought or given (the only staffing/budget communication in the record is the Barros email, in which Ms. Dominguez stated she wanted to "check where things stand against the current budget and staffing plan"). This is a § 7.3 violation.

**Treatment.** § 7.3 expressly provides that budget-compliance reductions "do not offset or preclude" the other Guidelines reductions. The specific reductions in Sections A–E above reduce payable attorney fees to **$123,032.49**, which is *below* the $189,750.00 ceiling. Accordingly, the § 7.3 cap is not the binding constraint, and **no additional dollar reduction is required under § 7.3** at this time. Pinnacle nonetheless **reserves the right to enforce the $189,750.00 ceiling**, and the violation should be documented and addressed: the Firm must obtain pre-approval before any future month exceeds the ceiling and should consider submitting a phase-budget adjustment request under § 7.4 if the elevated discovery volume is expected to persist.

---

### G. Expense Review (§ 8)

Request vendor invoice and disallow any expedited premium. |

| 4 | 05/15 | Courier / Delivery | Various | $500.00 | Allow (time-sensitive service/filing per § 8.1). |

#### Expense #2 — Travel ($2,100.00) — Disallowed

The entry seeks "Travel expenses for D. Hargrove and K. Cho to attend Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition — mileage, parking, meals." The Status Report states the Liu deposition "was taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia." Hargrove and Cho are H&L attorneys based at that Atlanta office. Travel to one's own office is local travel. The expense fails on three independent grounds:

1. **Local travel (§ 6.4):** "Local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances."
2. **No pre-approval (§ 8.3):** Aggregate travel exceeding $1,000 "must be pre-approved by the Designated In-House Counsel." $2,100 exceeds $1,000; no pre-approval is evidenced.
3. **Meals (§ 8.2):** Meals are reimbursable only "during approved out-of-town travel or working sessions extending past 8:00 p.m." Neither condition is met (the travel was local and no out-of-town travel was approved).

**Recommendation: disallow $2,100.00.**

**Expense reduction total: $2,100.00.** (Court-reporting and e-discovery items are flagged for documentation/verification rather than quantified reduction, pending the vendor invoices and pre-approval records.)

---

### H. Invoice Format — Missing Budget-to-Actual Reconciliation (§§ 7.2, 9.1(g))

§ 9.1(g) requires each invoice to include "[a] budget-to-actual reconciliation as required by § 7.2, showing both the current period and cumulative figures." § 7.2 further requires a "narrative explanation of any variance greater than ten percent (10%) from the midpoint of the approved monthly range." The approved midpoint is $130,000; as-billed fees of $193,372.50 represent a **~48.7% variance** above midpoint. The invoice contains no budget-to-actual reconciliation and no variance narrative. (The Status Report discusses case activity but does not provide the § 7.2 budget reconciliation or variance analysis, and is not part of the invoice.)

**Recommendation:** No dollar reduction, but the Firm should be required to **resubmit the invoice with the § 7.2 / § 9.1(g) budget-to-actual reconciliation** (current-period and cumulative figures, percentage of phase budget consumed, and a narrative explanation of the variance) before payment is released.

---

### I. Other Observations (no quantified reduction)

1. **Partner hours above expected range.** Hargrove billed 38.5 hours, above the Staffing Plan's expected 15–25 hrs/month. The Status Report attributes this to the Liu deposition, the May 27 client strategy session, and personal document review. The excess is addressed through the task-appropriateness (§ D.1–D.2) and budget (§ F) reductions; no separate reduction is recommended, but the Firm's projection that hours "will return to more typical levels in June and July" should be monitored.

2. **Multi-level review (§ 6.2).** Several work products passed through three levels (e.g., the motion to compel: Pettersson drafted → Cho reviewed → Hargrove reviewed/cite-checked). § 6.2 presumes a single supervisory review round is reasonable and treats additional rounds as requiring justification. For a $47.5 million trade-secret matter with a contested motion to compel, two review rounds are defensible, so no reduction is recommended; the Firm should be mindful of cumulative review costs in future months.

3. **Time-entry narratives (§ 9.2).** Narratives are generally adequately specific. Entry 79 ("Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege") is generic compared to the contract attorney's Bates-range-specific entries but identifies the production batch and is not so vague as to warrant rejection.

4. **Billing increments (§ 5.5).** All entries are recorded in 0.1-hour multiples (e.g., 0.5, 6.5); no impermissible quarter-hour increments were found.

5. **Rate caps (§ 5.1).** All timekeepers other than Tate are within their category caps (Hargrove $985 < $1,000; Cho $625 < $650; Pettersson/Barros $475 < $500; Webb/Sengupta $375 < $400).

Pinnacle reserves the right to enforce the ceiling.

---

## VII. Recommendations and Next Steps

1. **Approve payment of $129,382.49** (revised attorney fees $123,032.49 + revised expenses $6,350.00), and **withhold the remaining $72,440.01** pending resolution.

2. **Reject all fees for Nina Barros ($21,375.00) and Priya Sengupta ($10,312.50)** as unapproved timekeepers. If the Firm wishes any portion reconsidered, it must submit a written § 4.2 staffing-change request (name, role, rate, reason, anticipated hours, budget impact) and seek retroactive approval; Pinnacle retains discretion under § 4.2 to grant or deny.

3. **Re-rate Marcus Tate to $185/hr** and confirm the Redwood Document Solutions pass-through rate for all future invoices; the $210 rate is neither cap-compliant nor consistent with the approved Staffing Plan.

4. **Provide the Firm 15 days to resubmit the 44 block-billed entries** as separate, individually timed line items (§ 5.4). For any entry not timely resubmitted, the 25% reduction stands.

5. **Sustain the § 6.1 task-appropriateness reductions** (Entries 9, 43, 15, 79) and remind the Firm that document review, cite-checking, and administrative scheduling are not partner-level or attorney-billable tasks.

6. **Sustain the § 4.3 staffing reductions** and remind the Firm to (a) designate billing attendees in advance for any meeting exceeding three timekeepers and (b) request advance approval for any deposition requiring more than two billing attorneys at least five business days in advance.

7. **Disallow the $2,100.00 travel expense** and request the vendor invoice for the court-reporting charge (to confirm standard vs. expedited rates) and the pre-approval record for the e-discovery hosting charge.

8. **Require resubmission with the § 7.2 / § 9.1(g) budget-to-actual reconciliation** (current and cumulative figures, percentage of phase budget consumed, and a >10% variance narrative) before final payment processing.

9. **Address the § 7.3 budget overage:** notify the Firm in writing that May fees exceeded the 115% ceiling without pre-approval; require pre-approval before any future month exceeds the ceiling; and invite a § 7.4 phase-budget adjustment request if the elevated discovery volume is expected to continue through the September 30 close.

10. **Issue the reduction notice** through Pinnacle's e-billing system, itemizing each reduction by entry and authority so the Firm may exercise its § 10.3 appeal rights within 30 days.
